title: Yamê's 'Ebem' Is the Sound of Two Worlds Becoming One Language
author: Allastair Voss
body:
[spotify:album/4UPHNEDkuwpqz1GlcFOm2s]

## Neither and Both

Yamê is French. Yamê is Cameroonian. On Ebem, his debut album released to the kind of quiet critical reception that precedes a much louder second wave, he is neither trying to reconcile those identities nor keep them separate. He is doing something more interesting: letting them speak to each other in real time, across the same song, sometimes across the same bar.

Ebem means home in Bassa, one of the Cameroonian languages Yamê grew up around. The word also carries a second meaning: a communal space where wisdom passes between generations. The album uses that word as a question rather than an answer. Whose home? Which version of home? The one in Paris where Yamê built his career, or the one that lives in the music his family made before he was born? The album refuses to adjudicate. Both are home. The question is the answer.

The record is built on stories told by his grandmother, rooted in a specific family archive that predates his own memory. That source material is not decorative. It shapes the emotional temperature of the whole record, the sense that something essential is being carried forward, that the act of making this album is also the act of receiving something that was waiting to be received.

## Afropop Filtered Through French Melancholy

The production on Ebem occupies a genuinely unusual space. There are Cameroonian rhythmic structures, makossa influence, bikutsi patterns, that have been processed through the kind of atmospheric French pop production that Yamê absorbed growing up in Paris. The result does not sound like fusion, which is usually a polite word for compromise. It sounds like a native language that happens to be made of two others.

On Je Reviendrai, the guitar work is unmistakably West African in origin but the chord choices are Europop. The vocal sits between the two without apologizing for either. Yamê does not explain the junction. He inhabits it.

The track Shoot addresses escapism directly, but the escapism is not literal. The impulse to leave, to transform, to become something other than what your circumstances have assigned you, is treated as both necessary and insufficient. You can leave geographically. The weight travels with you. Yamê understands this with a specificity that is hard-earned and harder to fake.

The track Moto works metaphorical freedom against physical constraint, a recurring tension across the record. The production on that track is the sparsest on the album, which is the right call. The sparseness creates a kind of nakedness that the lyrical content requires.

That instinct, to present rather than explain, is the defining quality of Ebem as a record. Yamê is not making an album about being between cultures. He is making an album from inside that position, which is entirely different and far more valuable. The difference is everything.

## The Voice as Instrument

Yamê's voice is a baritone with unusual warmth in the lower registers. He uses it sparingly. On several tracks he barely pushes above a murmur, which means when he does open up, on the chorus of Douala, on the final minute of Mère, it lands harder than any technical display would.

The restraint is strategic. A voice this present in a room does not need to shout to fill it. Yamê understands that the listener leans in when the singer holds back, that the dynamic of withholding and releasing is as structural as a verse-chorus form. He learned this lesson early and applies it throughout the record without ever letting it feel like technique.

He has cited Stromae, Asa, and Keziah Jones as influences, and you can hear all three without any of them dominating. What Yamê has taken from each is not a sound but an attitude: the willingness to be emotionally direct without being sentimental, to be personal without being confessional, to make music that is clearly rooted in a specific life without closing the door on anyone who does not share it. That openness within specificity is the hardest thing to achieve and the thing that makes a record last.

## Why This Record Matters Now

The conversation about African artists in European markets has been dominated for years by Afrobeats, a genre that has proven its global reach definitively. But Afrobeats is one sound from one part of a continent that contains multitudes. Yamê is making music that draws from Central and West African traditions, makossa specifically, that rarely appear in that conversation, presented through a production sensibility shaped by Paris rather than Lagos or Accra.

That is not a criticism of Afrobeats. It is an observation about what Yamê is doing differently, and why Ebem deserves attention that is distinct from the Afrobeats conversation rather than adjacent to it. The African music that gets heard in European and North American markets still passes through a narrow set of genre filters. Yamê is neither fitting through nor refusing the filter. He is simply making the record the record needs to be, and the record needs to be something the filter wasn't designed to catch.

In 2025, Yamê received the grand jury prize at the Music Moves Europe Awards, recognition that arrived quietly but matters. It is the kind of institutional acknowledgment that opens doors without determining what you do once you walk through them. He walked through the door making exactly the music he was already making.

He is twenty-six. Ebem is a debut. Both of those facts feel almost impossible given how settled and self-possessed the record sounds. There is no searching here, no hedging, no effort to impress anyone with range. Just a fully formed voice addressing what it needs to address, in exactly the language it has to use.

The thing about Ebem that most review coverage missed is how the record breathes. It is not relentless. It does not pile achievement on achievement. There are passages of quiet, tracks that exist to let the listener rest before the next track asks something of them again. That structural intelligence, the understanding that a record is not just a collection of strong moments but a sequence, a pacing, a designed experience, is not common in debut albums. Yamê built a record, not just a set of songs. Pay attention to this name.
